title: Rush-woven chair back panel
subtitle: Figure-of-eight rush weave across a ladder-back chair frame
excerpt: A ladder-back chair back panel woven in dried bulrush: rush wound in a figure-of-eight pattern across the back rails, building up from the bottom rail to the top. The technique is the same used in traditional rush seating, applied to a narrower vertical panel. The result is firm, flexible, and repairable.
sourceNotes: Victorian rush seating and chair-making manuals; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for rush techniques; rush-seated furniture documented in museum collections at the Museum of English Rural Life.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dried stems of bulrush (Typha latifolia or Schoenoplectus lacustris) used for plaiting and weaving. Rush must be soaked before use to restore flexibility.
The standard rush-weaving wrap: the rush passes over the front rail, under, across to the opposite rail, over, under, and back. Each circuit locks the previous one in place.

content.text: A rush-woven chair back panel uses the same

seating, adapted to a narrower rectangular frame. Where a seat works outward from four corners and fills toward the centre, a back panel works upward from the bottom rail row by row until the top rail is reached. This makes it the easier introduction to rush weaving.

Cut bulrush in late summer and dry for 3 to 4 weeks before use. Before weaving, lay the stems on a wet hessian cloth, cover with a second damp cloth, and leave 20 minutes. The rush must be flexible enough to bend around the rail without cracking, but not so wet it becomes slimy. Sort by thickness; use thicker stems for the first and last rows, which carry the most strain.

Tie two rush stems together at their tips. Loop the joined end over the bottom rail at the left side and pull through until the knot sits on the top face of the rail, close to the left stile. The two stems run forward together as a paired unit.
Take the working pair over the bottom rail, under, and across to the top rail. Pass over the top rail, under, and back to the bottom rail to complete one circuit.
After each full circuit, use a flat piece of card or a bodkin to pack the new strand tightly against the previous one before moving on.
Join new rush by overlapping the old ends with new tips by 8 cm. Continue weaving with both until the join is locked by two rows; then trim the old ends close on the back face.
Continue until the panel is full. The last row finishes flush with or just below the top edge of the top rail. Tuck the working ends through the final two rows on the back and trim with secateurs.

Leave the finished panel to dry for 24 hours. As it dries the rush tightens and the weave firms. Do not attempt to adjust tension while drying. A thin coat of beeswax polish on the rails before weaving helps the rush slide into position without affecting the grip of the finished panel.

Mist lightly with water to revive a dry panel. Avoid prolonged direct sunlight, which bleaches and then brittles the rush over several seasons. Individual strands can be unpicked and re-woven without dismantling the whole panel, making rush weaving one of the more maintainable of the natural-craft traditions.

items.symptom: Visible gaps between rows when the panel has dried
items.cause: Rows not packed firmly against each other during weaving
items.fix: Use a flat piece of card or wide bodkin blade to compress each new row against the previous one before starting the next circuit. A panel that has gapped after drying can be unpicked back to the loose section and rewoven.
